title: Spider-Man Is Out of the MCU and Movies Are in a State of IP Crisis. Plus: Ben Berman on Untangling 'The Amazing Johnathan Documentary'
format: The Disney-Sony Spider-Man standoff kicks off a long, wide-ranging argument with Wesley Morris about IP, movie stars, and whether the middle of the movie business still exists. Plus: director Ben Berman on making The Amazing Johnathan Documentary.

segments.heading: Spider-Man, Sony, Disney, and the state of movie IP

films.title: The Matrix Resurrections
films.note: Announced this week, at the time untitled beyond 'The Matrix 4,' as a Lana Wachowski solo project after her creative split from sister Lily.

films.title: Beverly Hills Cop
films.note: Wesley's touchstone for the mid-80s complaint that action-comedy hybrids were dumbing down movies, via Pauline Kael's State of the Art.

films.title: Ghostbusters
films.note: Wesley's example of genre-on-genre construction, calling it 'a Marx Brothers movie with action imperatives.'

films.title: Us
films.note: Cited as the year's only top-15 movie that isn't a sequel, franchise entry, or adaptation, and Jordan Peele's proof that a filmmaker can build a personal brand strong enough to carry an original.

films.title: The Irishman
films.note: Wesley's case study for Scorsese's decades of brand-building meaning the film will be treated as an event no matter its quality.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Discussed via Bradley Cooper's refusal to explain the ending or campaign hard, which the group thinks may have cost him a directing nomination.

films.title: Cold War
films.note: Amazon's awards-campaign spending is credited with landing Pawel Pawlikowski a surprise Best Director nomination.

films.title: A Private War
films.note: Cited as the flip side: a strong Rosamund Pike performance nobody saw or campaigned for.

films.title: Mulan
films.note: Disney's live-action remake gets caught up in star Liu Yifei's social media comments supporting Hong Kong police during the protests.

films.title: Creed
films.note: Ryan Coogler's move into the Rocky franchise, which the group thinks was actually the riskier project since he had something new to say about it.

films.title: Zombieland: Double Tap
films.note: Cited as proof Emma Stone isn't immune to IP gravity even as a genuine movie star.

films.title: Passengers
films.note: An original studio movie with two stars that still flopped, cited as proof original star vehicles no longer have a built-in audience.

films.title: Kong: Skull Island
films.note: Brie Larson's post-Oscar franchise role, part of a case that she never really got a shot at movie stardom.

films.title: Captain Marvel
films.note: Larson's other big post-Oscar franchise role.

films.title: Unicorn Store
films.note: Larson's directorial debut, a personal passion project that landed quietly on Netflix.

films.title: The Glass Castle
films.note: Larson's other post-Oscar passion project, which didn't land.

films.title: Hobbs & Shaw
films.note: Vanessa Kirby's post-Crown breakout, offered as the more interesting career case against Claire Foy's.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Fallout
films.note: Also part of Kirby's rapid rise since The Crown.

films.title: Unsane
films.note: Claire Foy's Soderbergh detour after The Crown.

films.title: First Man
films.note: Discussed at length: a movie the culture had already decided not to engage with (a white man's story, again, at the wrong moment) that Wesley calls flatly great and thinks should have earned Foy an Oscar nomination.

films.title: The Girl in the Spider's Web
films.note: Foy's franchise misfire that nobody saw.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Cited as the last movie that could stage a true movie-star moment, in the Brad Pitt shirtless-on-the-roof scene.

films.title: The Nice Guys
films.note: The Russell Crowe/Ryan Gosling comedy the group wishes were coming out that weekend instead.

films.title: The Other Guys
films.note: The Will Ferrell/Mark Wahlberg comedy named in the same breath, mourned as evidence studios used to just try to make people laugh.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Adam McKay's expensive, divisive Cheney film, cited as the kind of mid-budget swing studios won't fund anymore.

films.title: Where'd You Go, Bernadette
films.note: Discussed as a flawed, uneven film that still earns a defense simply because it's not another franchise entry.

films.title: Queen & Slim
films.note: Cited as an upcoming original that Universal is betting money on, though its commercial fate is unclear.

films.title: Waves
films.note: Amanda's pick for what she's most excited about: a Trey Edward Shults film so sensory it resists plot summary.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Named as the kind of tidy, message-driven film Waves refuses to be.

films.title: Knives Out
films.note: Sean's pick: a Rian Johnson whodunit stacked with stars, discussed as a rare vehicle actually built around movie-star casting.

films.title: Clue
films.note: Brought up as the group debates homage versus parody ahead of Knives Out.

films.title: Uncut Gems
films.note: Wesley's pick, the new Safdie Brothers film he expects to finally win him over.

films.title: Good Time
films.note: The prior Safdie Brothers film that didn't fully work for Wesley.

films.title: The Lighthouse
films.note: Wesley's other big excitement: a Willem Dafoe/Robert Pattinson two-hander from Robert Eggers he calls 'deeply damaged and fascinating.'

films.title: Nightingale
films.note: Discussed at length as one of the year's best-directed films, a relentlessly violent revisionist Western that Wesley says nobody's talking about.

films.title: The Babadook
films.note: Named as Jennifer Kent's first film, the reason Amanda admits she's been avoiding Nightingale.

films.title: Roma
films.note: The reference point for whether Parasite can repeat a foreign-language Best Picture run, discussed as a film some Academy members felt was 'jammed down their throats.'

films.title: Parasite
films.note: Bong Joon-ho's Cannes winner, framed as building toward a real Best Picture push.

films.title: Zama
films.note: Named as maybe Lucrecia Martel's best film, cited as an example of a great director getting no attention.

films.title: Gemini Man
films.note: Ang Lee's upcoming film, mentioned alongside his prior swing as evidence even acclaimed directors get written off before release.

films.title: Pain and Glory
films.note: Almodovar's upcoming film, cited as proof it took him decades to earn the standing to keep making personal films.

films.title: Fighting with My Family
films.note: Wesley watched the whole thing on a plane for Florence Pugh, and dwells on the odd sight of Vince Vaughn and Dwayne Johnson playing old friends.

segments.heading: Ben Berman on making The Amazing Johnathan Documentary

films.title: The Amazing Johnathan Documentary
films.note: Berman's Sundance documentary about magician the Amazing Johnathan, which spirals once a second film crew shows up to make a competing doc about the same dying subject.

films.title: F for Fake
films.note: Orson Welles's essay film on forgery is a late discovered touchstone; its closing trick, admitting the last 30 minutes were a lie, is one Berman cites directly.

films.title: Minding the Gap
films.note: Berman's favorite film of the previous year, and part of why he wanted to go with Hulu.

films.title: The Florida Project
films.note: The last thing Berman rewatched, unrelated to the interview's main subject.

referenced.group: In passing during the IP discussion
Named in the Ben Berman interview

TV: Tim and Eric Awesome Show, Great Job!

The Girl in the Spider's Web.title: The Girl in the Spider's Web

Once Upon a Time in Hollywood.title: Once Upon a Time... in Hollywood
